author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Seeing the world as a machine arose from better understanding physics through modeling, and being able to use those models to do useful things.

The mechanical universe began to fade away with the uncertanty and random nature of quantum dynamics. But  there is a growing trend to seeing the universe as a perfect quantum computer performing perfect quantum calclations in real time. Maybe one might call it a simulation, that may or may not have been intelegently designed. 

Some aspects of quantum dynamics even sugest the posibility of multiple universes with different laws of physics.  Supposing that were so, it begins to blow one's mind to contemplate the nature of a thing that runs a multitude of physics simulations.

As used here, computer, and run program, and do caculations are simply  analogies that we might relate to.

author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text: I've experimented with auxetics a bit myself, it's quite fun to explore what's possible, and to build on that. Often I try to incorporate flexibility into parts of my designs, to alleviate stresses and allow fittings, but useful mechanical motion is hard to hone in on despite the abundance of potential for it! Have you seen those flexing elephants? At this point it's like using electrons as billions of hinges lol, so below plastic-deformation there's way less wear. Though the maths of the restraint of either side of a flexure, for precision, is a mystery I look forwards to finding the time to solve! :)

author: @mattmill30
text:
I didn't get out of the video a meaningful definition for a "compliant mechanism". Are you able to provide a one-line definition, beyond "a compliant mechanism is a flexible mechanism". How does the word "compliant" relate to "flexible"?
Or is it that due to it's flexibility it can change it's condition, or functional state, without changing it's mechanical state?
e.g. a rubber band in a sling shot

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: In mechanical engineering, a compliant mechanism is a flexible mechanism that achieves force and motion transmission through elastic body deformation

replies.author: @mattmill30
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering so why the word "compliant"? does it mean "single-state mechanism"? and "non-compliant" would apply to a "multi-state mechanism"?

replies.author: @BuddyGorey
@mattmill30
replies.text: @BuddyGorey isn't the "stability of a flexible part" it's resilience or elasticity, with a "permanent change in shape or size when subjected to a stress" being it's plasticity?

replies.author: @BuddyGorey
replies.text:
@mattmill30  exactly! Resilience in elasticity, or the relationship between those two… and plasticity seems like it’d be a separate related measurement. Maybe the starting point. Birttleness… I suppose that’s the same scale. You nailed it though. I’m dumbstruck about how you’d begin to determine those things reliably though. 

It feels more complex than the measurements for strength under load, shearing strength. The movement changes everything— other than steel cables on bridges, I can’t say I’ve ever thought about anything built with bending structural parts. It would depend, to use the tree analogy, on not just the type of wood, but the thickness of the branch. And where the load is placed, of course.

Even eliminating the more chaotic elements of natural materials, the way drought cycles would effect wood density and so on, it seems like you’d have to start at zero with every material. Determining a base unit of measurement, a strand size or some such, and going on from there. Reveling in my ignorance really. Hoping there’s more coming soon on this. 

Matt if your clarity about elasticity and plasticity come from experience with this kind of thing or thinking, or even if not, welcome any insight. 

Maybe there’s a simple way to measure these things and it’s only unfamiliarity that’s made it opaque to me.
